title: Abuse of Dominance ‚Äî Market Definition
content:
Session 6: Abuse of dominant position [¬ß¬ß2(r), 2(s), 2(t), 19(4), 19(5), 19 (6) and  19(7) of the 
Competition Act] 
CCI v Schott Glass [2025 | SC | 2 J] 
Facts: 
‚ùñ‚Äã Parties 
‚û¢‚Äã Schott Glass India Pvt. Ltd. (‚ÄúSchott India‚Äù): Manufacturer of neutral USP-1 borosilicate glass tubing, a critical 
input used for manufacturing pharmaceutical containers such as ampoules, vials, cartridges, and syringes. 
‚û¢‚Äã Kapoor Glass Pvt. Ltd. (‚ÄúKapoor Glass‚Äù): A downstream converter manufacturing pharmaceutical containers, 
procuring borosilicate glass tubing from Schott India. 
‚û¢‚Äã Schott Kaisha: A competing converter, structured as a joint venture (50:50) between Schott AG (global parent) and 
Kaisha Manufacturers. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Relevant Markets 
‚û¢‚Äã Upstream Market: Manufacture and sale of neutral USP-1 borosilicate glass tubing in India. 
‚û¢‚Äã Downstream Market: Manufacture and sale of pharmaceutical containers (ampoules, vials, cartridges, syringes). 
‚ùñ‚Äã Allegations by Kapoor Glass 
‚û¢‚Äã Kapoor Glass alleged that Schott India abused its dominant position by: 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Discriminatory pricing and margin squeeze: Additional rebates and favourable contractual terms given to 
Schott Kaisha resulted in a margin squeeze under Section 4(2)(e). 
‚û¢‚Äã Unfair conditions (functional rebates) ‚Äì Conditioning functional rebates on: 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Non-processing of ‚ÄúChinese tubing‚Äù 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Compliance with ‚Äúfair pricing‚Äù norms 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Execution of a Trademark Licence Agreement (TMLA) 
‚û¢‚Äã Tying/Bundling: 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Rebates were contingent on achieving combined purchase targets of two variants of tubing‚ÄîNGA 
(amber) and NGC (clear)‚Äîallegedly amounting to tying under Section 4(2)(d). 
126 
 
‚ùñ‚Äã Procedural History 
‚û¢‚Äã 2010: Kapoor Glass filed an information before the CCI. 
‚û¢‚Äã DG Investigation: Based largely on statements of 19 converters selected by the informant. 
‚û¢‚Äã CCI Order: 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Found Schott India guilty of abuse of dominance. 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Imposed a penalty of INR 56.6 million and issued a cease-and-desist order. 
‚û¢‚Äã COMPAT: 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Reversed the CCI‚Äôs findings. 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Imposed costs of INR 100,000 on Kapoor Glass. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Appeal to Supreme Court: 
 
Issues: 
‚ùñ‚Äã Whether uniform, volume-based rebate schemes amount to discriminatory pricing under Section 4(2)(a) or exclusionary 
conduct under Section 4(2)(b)? 
‚ùñ‚Äã Whether functional rebates conditional upon quality, safety, and brand-protection requirements constitute unfair or 
discriminatory conditions? 
‚ùñ‚Äã Whether the Long-Term Tubing Supply Agreement (LTTSA) between Schott India and Schott Kaisha resulted in a margin 
squeeze under Section 4(2)(e)? 
‚ùñ‚Äã Whether aggregation of NGA and NGC tubing volumes for rebates amounts to tying or bundling under Section 4(2)(d)? 
‚ùñ‚Äã Whether an effects-based (harm) analysis is mandatory in abuse of dominance cases under Section 4? 
‚ùñ‚Äã Whether denial of cross-examination vitiated the DG investigation and the CCI order on grounds of natural justice? 
 
Rule: 
‚ùñ‚Äã Abuse of Dominance (Section 4) 
‚û¢‚Äã Section 4 does not prohibit dominance per se, but only abuse of dominance. 
‚û¢‚Äã Conduct must: 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Fall within one of the categories under Section 4(2)(a)‚Äì(e); and 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Cause or be likely to cause Appreciable Adverse Effect on Competition (AAEC). 
‚ùñ‚Äã Price Discrimination & Rebates (Section 4(2)(a)) 
‚û¢‚Äã Uniform, transparent, and volume-based rebates: 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Are permissible if offered to all similarly placed purchasers. 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Do not constitute discrimination if differences are objectively justified by economies of scale and efficiency 
gains. 
‚û¢‚Äã Differential timing of rebates without differential pricing does not amount to discrimination. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Exclusionary Conduct (Section 4(2)(b)) 
‚û¢‚Äã Mere theoretical foreclosure is insufficient. 
‚û¢‚Äã There must be evidence of actual or likely exclusion, limitation of output, or foreclosure of competitors. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Margin Squeeze (Section 4(2)(e)) 
‚û¢‚Äã Adopting TeliaSonera (ECJ), margin squeeze requires proof that: 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
The dominant firm is present in the downstream market; 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
The price spread is insufficient for an equally efficient competitor to survive; and 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
The conduct threatens competitive harm or foreclosure. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Tying/Bundling (Section 4(2)(d)) 
‚û¢‚Äã Tying exists only where: 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
There are two distinct products; 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Purchase of the tying product is conditional upon purchase of the tied product; and 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
There is foreclosure in the tied product market. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Effects-Based Analysis 
‚û¢‚Äã Competitive harm must be shown through actual evidence, not presumptions. 
127 
 
‚û¢‚Äã Pro-competitive efficiencies must be weighed under Section 19(4). 
‚û¢‚Äã Section 4 contains no deeming provision of abuse. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Natural Justice 
‚û¢‚Äã Under Section 36(2) and Regulation 41, denial of cross-examination where oral evidence is relied upon causes 
serious prejudice and vitiates proceedings. 
 
Application: 
‚ùñ‚Äã Issue 1: Volume-Based Rebates 
‚û¢‚Äã Schott India circulated a single rebate ladder (2%, 5%, 8%, 12%) applicable uniformly. 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Rebates depended only on aggregate annual volume, irrespective of buyer identity. 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Schott Kaisha received higher rebates solely due to higher volumes. 
‚û¢‚Äã Efficiency justifications (economies of scale, furnace optimisation) were proven. 
‚û¢‚Äã No evidence of foreclosure, stable container prices, and expanding supply. 
‚û¢‚Äã SO ‚Üí No violation of Sections 4(2)(a) or 4(2)(b). 
‚ùñ‚Äã Issue 2: Functional Rebates 
‚û¢‚Äã Rebates linked to: 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Annual purchase targets 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Avoidance of Chinese tubing (patient safety) 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Brand integrity via TMLA 
‚û¢‚Äã TMLA granted royalty-free trademark usage with safeguards (inspection rights, bank guarantee). 
‚û¢‚Äã No evidence of differential treatment between similarly placed converters. 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
No adverse effect on upstream or downstream markets. 
‚û¢‚Äã Objectively justified, proportionate, non-discriminatory. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Issue 3: Margin Squeeze 
‚û¢‚Äã Schott India is not active in the downstream market; Schott Kaisha is legally independent. 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Independent converters showed rising profits and margins. 
‚û¢‚Äã No foreclosure: imports increased, competitors expanded capacity. 
‚û¢‚Äã LTTSA justified continuous furnace economics and capital investment needs. 
‚û¢‚Äã Margin squeeze not established. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Issue 4: Tying/Bundling 
‚û¢‚Äã NGA and NGC: 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Produced from same furnace 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Differ only by iron oxide addition 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
No independent consumer demand for NGA 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
No contractual compulsion to purchase both. 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Aggregation was merely a multi-product volume discount. 
‚û¢‚Äã No foreclosure or output restriction. 
‚û¢‚Äã No tying under Section 4(2)(d). 
‚ùñ‚Äã Issue 5: Effects-Based Analysis 
‚û¢‚Äã CCI failed to assess: 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Actual foreclosure 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Impact on prices, output, or consumer welfare 
‚û¢‚Äã Evidence showed: 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Independent converters prospered 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Buyers paid equal or higher prices 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Reliance on theoretical harm rejected. 
‚û¢‚Äã The Supreme Court affirms effects-based approach as mandatory. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Issue 6: Natural Justice 
‚û¢‚Äã DG relied on statements of hostile witnesses without cross-examination. 
128 
 
‚û¢‚Äã CCI rejected cross-examination on technical grounds. 
‚û¢‚Äã Violated audi alteram partem. 
‚û¢‚Äã Consistent with Cadila Healthcare and settled SC precedent. 
‚û¢‚Äã Proceedings vitiated by procedural unfairness. 
 
Conclusion: 
‚ùñ‚Äã The Supreme Court dismissed CCI‚Äôs appeal and upheld the COMPAT order. 
‚ùñ‚Äã No abuse of dominance by Schott India under Sections 4(2)(a), (b), (d), or (e). 
‚ùñ‚Äã Reaffirmed: 
‚û¢‚Äã Centrality of effects-based analysis 
‚û¢‚Äã Legality of efficiency-driven rebate schemes 
‚û¢‚Äã Importance of procedural fairness 
‚û¢‚Äã Warned against chilling legitimate business conduct. 
‚û¢‚Äã Positioned Indian competition law in alignment with EU jurisprudence. 
 
CCI v. Fast Way Transmission Pvt. Ltd. [2018 | SC | 2 J] 
Facts: 
‚ùñ‚Äã The dispute arose in the Cable Television services sector in Punjab and Chandigarh, involving four levels of market 
participants: broadcasters, aggregators, Multi System Operators (MSOs), and Last Mile Cable Operators (LCOs).  
‚û¢‚Äã The informant, Kansan News Pvt. Ltd., operated a news and current affairs television channel titled ‚ÄúDay and 
Night News.‚Äù  
‚û¢‚Äã OPs were a group of MSOs operating in Punjab and Chandigarh, including Fast Way Transmission Pvt. Ltd., who 
collectively controlled a significant portion of cable distribution infrastructure in the region. 
‚ùñ‚Äã The informant entered into a broadcast carriage agreement with the MSOs for a fixed period of one year commencing from 
1 August 2010.  
‚û¢‚Äã However, during the subsistence of this agreement, the MSOs issued termination notices dated 19 January 2011, 
effectively discontinuing carriage of the informant‚Äôs channel prior to expiry of the contractual term 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Alleged that this termination was illegal, discriminatory, and motivated by political considerations, 
contending that the MSOs were aligned with the ruling political party in Punjab and were hostile to the 
broadcaster‚Äôs editorial stance. 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Also complained MSOs were demanding higher carriage fees which the broadcaster was unable to pay, 
following which the termination was effected ‚Üí amounted to a denial of market access, since the MSOs 
controlled the principal means of cable distribution in the region.  
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Additional allegations of discriminatory conditions vis-√Ý-vis other broadcasters were also raised. 
‚ùñ‚Äã An information was filed before the CCI alleging violations of cartelisation and refusal to deal and abuse of dominant 
position.  
‚û¢‚Äã The relevant market was identified as Cable TV services in Punjab and Chandigarh, and the MSO group was found 
to hold approximately 85% market share in terms of subscribers.  
‚û¢‚Äã The MSOs were also found to be part of the same corporate group, constituting a single economic entity for 
competition law purposes. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Parallelly, the matter also came before the TDSAT, which found that the termination violated Regulation 4.2 of the relevant 
Telecom Regulations, as no reasons were provided in the termination notices. 
 
Issues: 
‚ùñ‚Äã Whether denial of market access under Section 4(2)(c) requires the victim to be a competitor in the same relevant market as 
the dominant enterprise. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Whether unlawful termination of a broadcast carriage agreement by a dominant MSO amounts to denial of market access 
under Section 4(2)(c). 
129 
 
‚ùñ‚Äã Whether, despite a finding of abuse of dominance, a penalty must necessarily be imposed, or whether an ‚Äúotherwise 
justifiable‚Äù defence can preclude penalties. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Whether the CCI‚Äôs jurisdiction is excluded or limited due to the presence of a sectoral regulator (TDSAT). 
 
Rules: 
‚ùñ‚Äã Section 4 ‚Üí prohibits abuse of dominant position.  
‚û¢‚Äã Specifically, Section 4(2)(c) provides that there shall be an abuse of dominance if a dominant enterprise ‚Äúdenies 
market access in any manner.‚Äù 
‚û¢‚Äã The phrase ‚Äúin any manner [amended] indicates a broad and inclusive legislative intent.  
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Unlike Section 4(2)(a), which focuses on unfair or discriminatory conditions within the same market, 
Section 4(2)(c) does not explicitly confine its application to competitors operating at the same level of the 
supply chain. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Section 19(4) provides factors for assessing dominance, including market share, size and resources, dependence of 
consumers, and ability to operate independently of competitive forces. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Section 60 provides an overriding effect to the Competition Act in case of inconsistency with other laws. 
 
Application: 
‚ùñ‚Äã Dominant Position and Relevant Market 
‚û¢‚Äã SC noted that the finding of dominance was never overturned by COMPAT.  
‚ñÝ‚Äã
The MSO group‚Äôs 85% subscriber share, combined with its control over cable infrastructure, consumer 
dependence, lack of substitutes, and ability to influence the market structure, clearly established a 
dominant position in the relevant market of Cable TV services in Punjab and Chandigarh ‚Üí satisfied the 
threshold requirement under Section 4. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Denial of Market Access under Section 4(2)(c) 
‚û¢‚Äã COMPAT ‚Üí denial of market access can only occur vis-√Ý-vis competitors, reasoning that the purpose of 
competition law is to protect competition between rival firms operating in the same market.  
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Since broadcasters and MSOs operate at different levels of the supply chain ‚Üí 4(2)(c) could not apply. 
‚û¢‚Äã SC rejected this ‚Üí statutory language [‚Äúin any manner‚Äù] is of wide import and must be given its natural meaning.  
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Denial of market access need not be confined to the same relevant market, and can extend to downstream 
or upstream markets, so long as the conduct of the dominant enterprise forecloses access to a market. 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Once dominance is established, the question of whether the victim enterprise is a competitor becomes 
irrelevant for the purposes of Section 4(2)(c).  
‚óè‚Äã
What matters is the effect of the conduct ‚Üí whether access to the market has been denied due to 
the dominant enterprise‚Äôs actions.  
‚û¢‚Äã In this case, the broadcaster was entirely dependent on the MSO group for access to cable subscribers in Punjab.  
‚ñÝ‚Äã
The premature and unlawful termination of the agreement effectively excluded the broadcaster from the 
market for the remaining contractual period. 
‚û¢‚Äã The Court relied significantly on the TDSAT‚Äôs finding that the termination violated Telecom Regulations due to 
lack of reasons.  
‚ñÝ‚Äã
This illegality strengthened the conclusion that the termination was not merely contractual, but had 
competition-foreclosing effects, thereby constituting abuse of dominance. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Reconciling the ‚ÄúCompetitor‚Äù Objection 
‚û¢‚Äã Section 4(2)(c) is concerned not merely with competition within a market, but with control over access to a market 
itself.  
‚ñÝ‚Äã
In vertically structured industries, dominant firms can abuse control over essential facilities even against 
non-competitors.  
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Thus, the broadcaster need not be a competitor of the MSO; it is sufficient that the MSO‚Äôs conduct 
prevents participation in the market altogether. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Penalty and the ‚ÄúOtherwise Justifiable‚Äù Defence 
130 
 
‚û¢‚Äã Despite affirming a clear violation of Section 4(2)(c), the SC set aside the penalty imposed by the CCI.  
‚û¢‚Äã The Court examined the MSOs‚Äô justification for termination [the poor TRP/GRP ratings of the informant‚Äôs 
channel]  
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Contrary to the CCI‚Äôs finding, the Court observed that the GRP of ‚ÄúDay and Night News‚Äù was 
substantially lower than that of other channels, making it commercially unattractive ‚Üí although the 
termination was procedurally unlawful and thus amounted to denial of market access, the underlying 
commercial rationale was ‚Äúotherwise justifiable.‚Äù ‚áí no penalty ought to be imposed, even though abuse 
of dominance was established. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Objective or justifiable reasons do not negate the finding of abuse, but can operate only at the penalty stage.  
‚û¢‚Äã Fast Way does not hold that objective justification erases abuse; rather, it limits the consequences of such abuse. 
 
Conclusion: 
‚ùñ‚Äã Section 4(2)(c) applies even where the denial of market access occurs in a downstream market, and the victim enterprise need 
not be a competitor in the same relevant market. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Unlawful termination of a broadcast carriage agreement by a dominant MSO amounts to denial of market access, 
constituting abuse of dominance. 
‚ùñ‚Äã However, where the conduct is supported by otherwise justifiable commercial reasons, no penalty need be imposed, despite 
the existence of abuse. 
‚ùñ‚Äã The CCI‚Äôs jurisdiction is not ousted by sectoral regulation, and the Competition Act retains the overriding effect under 
Section 60. 
 
Google v Payment Aggregator (Alphabet Inc. v CCI) [2023 | NCLAT] 
Facts: 
‚ùñ‚Äã Google‚Äôs Play Store Business Model 
‚û¢‚Äã Google operates the Google Play Store, which is the primary app distribution platform for Android devices. 
‚û¢‚Äã App developers who wish to distribute paid apps or offer in-app purchases (IAPs) on the Play Store are required to 
comply with Google‚Äôs billing and payment policies. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Mandatory Use of Google Play Billing System (GPBS) 
‚û¢‚Äã Google mandated that all app developers offering paid apps or in-app purchases on the Play Store must exclusively 
use Google Play Billing System (GPBS). 
‚û¢‚Äã Developers were prohibited from using alternative payment processors, including: 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
UPI apps (PhonePe, Paytm, etc.) 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Net banking 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Mobile wallets 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Credit/debit cards via third-party gateways 
‚û¢‚Äã Google charged a commission of 15%‚Äì30% on transactions processed through GPBS. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Allegations Against Google ‚Üí Mandatory GPBS constituted: 
‚û¢‚Äã Unfair and one-sided contractual conditions 
‚û¢‚Äã Exclusion of rival payment aggregators 
‚û¢‚Äã Leveraging of dominance from Android OS and Play Store markets into the UPI/payment facilitation market 
‚ùñ‚Äã Google allegedly: 
‚û¢‚Äã Did not charge commission for payments made through its own apps (e.g., YouTube), resulting in discriminatory 
treatment 
‚û¢‚Äã Used different technical integration methodologies for its own UPI app (Google Pay) vis-√Ý-vis rival UPI apps. 
‚ùñ‚Äã CCI‚Äôs Findings ‚Äì held Google guilty of abuse of dominance under: 
‚û¢‚Äã Section 4(2)(a)(i) ‚Äì unfair terms and conditions 
‚û¢‚Äã Section 4(2)(a)(ii) ‚Äì discriminatory conditions 
‚û¢‚Äã Section 4(2)(c) ‚Äì denial of market access 
‚û¢‚Äã Section 4(2)(e) ‚Äì leveraging 
131 
 
‚ùñ‚Äã Penalty imposed: ‚Çπ936 crore 
‚ùñ‚Äã Google challenged the CCI‚Äôs findings in entirety before the NCLAT. 
 
Issues: 
‚ùñ‚Äã What are the relevant markets affected by Google‚Äôs conduct? 
‚ùñ‚Äã Is Google dominant in the relevant markets? 
‚ùñ‚Äã Does mandatory use of GPBS amount to: 
‚û¢‚Äã Unfair conditions under Section 4(2)(a)(i)? 
‚û¢‚Äã Discriminatory treatment under Section 4(2)(a)(ii)? 
‚ùñ‚Äã Does mandatory GPBS result in: 
‚û¢‚Äã Denial of market access under Section 4(2)(c)? 
‚û¢‚Äã Limitation of technical or scientific development under Section 4(2)(b)(ii)? 
‚ùñ‚Äã Does Google leverage its dominance in OS and app store markets to enter or protect its position in payment processing 
under Section 4(2)(e)? 
‚ùñ‚Äã Is an effects-based analysis required under Section 4? 
‚ùñ‚Äã Is CCI engaging in impermissible ex ante ‚Äúgatekeeper‚Äù regulation? 
‚ùñ‚Äã What is the appropriate basis and quantum for penalty? 
 
Rules: 
‚ùñ‚Äã Section 4, Competition Act, 2002 ‚Äì Abuse of dominant position includes: 
‚û¢‚Äã Imposition of unfair or discriminatory conditions [4(2)(a)] 
‚û¢‚Äã Limiting technical or scientific development [4(2)(b)(ii)] 
‚û¢‚Äã Denial of market access [4(2)(c)] 
‚û¢‚Äã Leveraging dominance in one market to enter or protect another [4(2)(e)] 
‚ùñ‚Äã Relevant Market Definition 
‚û¢‚Äã Determined by demand-side and supply-side substitutability 
‚û¢‚Äã Multi-homing ‚âÝ demand substitution (MMT-Go logic) 
‚ùñ‚Äã Effects-Based Analysis 
‚û¢‚Äã Abuse must be shown through: 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Actual harm, and 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Conduct capable of causing harm 
‚û¢‚Äã However, conduct must have already occurred, not merely be speculative. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Ex-post Nature of Competition Act 
‚û¢‚Äã CCI‚Äôs jurisdiction under Section 4 is corrective, not regulatory. 
‚û¢‚Äã No concept of ‚Äúgatekeeper‚Äù obligations unless legislatively introduced. 
 
Application: 
‚ùñ‚Äã Whether Mandatory Use of Google Play Billing System (GPBS) Constitutes Unfair Conditions under Section 
4(2)(a)(i) 
‚û¢‚Äã The Commission‚Äôs analysis proceeded from the finding that Google was dominant in the relevant market for app 
stores for Android OS in India.  
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Given that the Play Store is pre-installed on virtually all Android devices and functions as an unavoidable 
trading partner for app developers seeking access to Android users, the Commission characterised Google 
as exercising gatekeeper-like control over access to the market. 
‚û¢‚Äã THUS, mandating the exclusive use of GPBS deprived app developers of any real choice with respect to payment 
processing.  
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Developers were not merely incentivised but compelled to use Google‚Äôs billing system, irrespective of 
whether alternative payment processors offered lower transaction costs, better innovation, or services 
more suited to the Indian payments ecosystem.  
132 
 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
This lack of choice was compounded by the fact that users, too, were conditioned to transact only 
through GPBS for paid apps and in-app purchases, thereby foreclosing alternative payment options at 
both ends of the market.  
‚óè‚Äã
The unilateral imposition of such a condition by a dominant platform was therefore found to be 
inherently unfair within the meaning of Section 4(2)(a)(i). 
‚û¢‚Äã On appeal, the NCLAT substantially affirmed this finding.  
‚ñÝ‚Äã
The mandatory nature of GPBS, when imposed by an enterprise dominant in the app store market, 
resulted in unfair conditions for app developers.  
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Importantly, the Tribunal accepted the Commission‚Äôs effects-based reasoning that unfairness under 
Section 4(2)(a)(i) does not require proof of actual harm alone; it is sufficient if the conduct is capable of 
causing harm to competition or to the freedom of market participants.  
‚óè‚Äã
The NCLAT clarified, however, that such effects analysis must be confined to conduct that has 
already occurred, and not speculative or future conduct.  
‚óè‚Äã
Since the mandatory GPBS policy had already been implemented and enforced, the requirement 
of an effects-based inquiry was satisfied. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Whether Differential Treatment of Google‚Äôs Own Apps Constituted Discriminatory Conduct under Section 
4(2)(a)(ii) 
‚û¢‚Äã A related allegation concerned discriminatory treatment, namely that Google did not subject its own apps, 
particularly YouTube, to the same 15‚Äì30% service fee imposed on third-party app developers using GPBS ‚Üí prima 
facie discriminatory, noting that Google‚Äôs internal monetisation arrangements effectively insulated its own apps 
from costs that rival developers were compelled to bear.  
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Such differential treatment distorted the competitive playing field and violated Section 4(2)(a)(ii). 
‚û¢‚Äã The NCLAT, however, reversed this finding. It drew a clear distinction between transactions involving third-party 
developers and intra-group transactions within Google‚Äôs own corporate structure.  
‚ñÝ‚Äã
YouTube was a wholly-owned Google entity, and any payment routed through GPBS in respect of 
YouTube subscriptions would effectively amount to Google paying itself.  
‚óè‚Äã
In such circumstances, the absence of a commission charge could not be equated with 
discriminatory pricing vis-√Ý-vis independent developers.  
‚ñÝ‚Äã
The CCI itself had acknowledged the lack of sufficient information regarding Google‚Äôs overall 
monetisation model, which further weakened the basis for a finding of discrimination.  
‚û¢‚Äã Consequently, the NCLAT held that no contravention of Section 4(2)(a)(ii) was made out on this count. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Whether Mandatory GPBS Restricted Technical or Scientific Development under Section 4(2)(b)(ii) 
‚û¢‚Äã The Commission further examined whether Google‚Äôs conduct had the effect of limiting or restricting technical or 
scientific development in the market for payment processors and aggregators, in violation of Section 4(2)(b)(ii).  
‚ñÝ‚Äã
The CCI accepted the DG‚Äôs theory of harm that by excluding third-party payment processors from in-app 
payment flows on the Play Store, Google reduced incentives for innovation in payment technologies and 
foreclosed competitive development in this adjacent market. 
‚û¢‚Äã The NCLAT rejected this conclusion ‚Üí placed significant reliance on empirical considerations ‚Üí payments 
processed through GPBS constituted less than 1% of the total digital payments ecosystem in India.  
‚ñÝ‚Äã
In light of the rapid and sustained growth of UPI-based payment solutions across the country ‚Üí 
implausible to suggest that innovation in payment processing had been materially restricted by Google‚Äôs 
conduct.  
‚ñÝ‚Äã
The relevant market for payment processors had not been formally delineated by the Commission, 
rendering the finding under Section 4(2)(b)(ii) analytically unsustainable.  
‚û¢‚Äã Accordingly, this head of abuse was set aside. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Whether Google‚Äôs Conduct Resulted in Denial of Market Access under Section 4(2)(c) 
‚û¢‚Äã The CCI held that by making GPBS the exclusive route for in-app payments, Google denied rival payment 
aggregators access to a significant channel of demand, thereby violating Section 4(2)(c). 
‚û¢‚Äã The NCLAT disagreed with this assessment.  
133 
 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Denial of market access must be assessed in the context of the overall market structure.  
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Since more than 99% of digital payments in India occurred outside the Play Store ecosystem, and payment 
processors remained free to compete for transactions across the broader UPI and digital payments market, 
the Tribunal concluded that there was no effective foreclosure.  
‚ñÝ‚Äã
The limited subset of transactions governed by GPBS was insufficient, in the Tribunal‚Äôs view, to 
constitute denial of market access within the meaning of Section 4(2)(c). 
‚ùñ‚Äã Whether Google Leveraged Its Dominance in the App Store and OS Markets in Violation of Section 4(2)(e) 
‚û¢‚Äã The Tribunal accepted that Google was dominant in two upstream markets: the market for licensable mobile 
operating systems for smart devices in India, and the market for app stores for Android OS + Google had used this 
dominance to protect and strengthen its position in the downstream market for payment processing services. 
‚û¢‚Äã In reaching this conclusion, the NCLAT relied on the statutory language of Section 4(2)(e), which contemplates 
the use of dominance in one market to enter into or protect another market.  
‚ñÝ‚Äã
By making access to Android users conditional upon compliance with GPBS, Google effectively 
compelled app developers to adopt its payment solution, thereby reinforcing its presence in the payments 
space.  
‚ñÝ‚Äã
This form of dependency-based leveraging was held to fall squarely within the mischief sought to be 
addressed by Section 4(2)(e), notwithstanding the relatively small share of Google Play transactions in the 
overall payments market. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Ex Ante Regulation, Gatekeeper Characterisation, and Limits of CCI‚Äôs Powers 
‚û¢‚Äã Google argued that the CCI‚Äôs directions amounted to impermissible ex ante regulation, particularly by 
characterising Google as a ‚Äúgatekeeper‚Äù and imposing special responsibilities akin to those contemplated under 
proposed digital competition frameworks.  
‚û¢‚Äã The NCLAT accepted this submission in part, holding that under the existing Competition Act, the CCI operates 
on an ex post facto enforcement model. 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Merely labelling an enterprise as a gatekeeper cannot substitute for a finding of abuse under Section 4, nor 
can it justify forward-looking regulatory directions.  
‚ñÝ‚Äã
The Tribunal clarified that such an approach might be permissible under a future Digital Competition 
Act, but not under the current statutory framework. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Penalty 
‚û¢‚Äã Finally, the NCLAT modified the penalty imposed by the CCI.  
‚û¢‚Äã While the Commission had levied a penalty of INR 936 crore based on Google‚Äôs total turnover in India, the 
Tribunal held that the penalty must be linked to the relevant turnover arising from the infringing conduct.  
‚û¢‚Äã Applying a rate of 7% to the average relevant turnover for the preceding three financial years, the NCLAT reduced 
the penalty to approximately INR 216.69 crore.  
‚û¢‚Äã The Tribunal also noted that the 2024 Penalty Guidelines were not applicable retrospectively. 
 
MCX v National Stock Exchange [2011 | CCI ]  
Facts: 
‚ùñ‚Äã NSE operates multiple segments of stock exchange services, including the currency derivatives (CD) segment, equity 
segment, and futures and options segment.  
‚ùñ‚Äã Multi Commodity Exchange Stock Exchange of India Ltd. (MCX-SX) entered the CD segment as a competitor to NSE ‚Üí 
filed an information under Section 19(1)(a): 
‚ùñ‚Äã alleging that NSE had abused its dominant position by offering zero transaction fees and waiving admission fees in the CD 
segment.  
‚ùñ‚Äã NSE also allegedly kept membership deposits unjustifiably low.  
‚ùñ‚Äã According to the informant, these pricing practices were not commercially viable and were adopted as an exclusionary 
strategy to eliminate competition in the CD segment, particularly MCX-SX. 
‚ùñ‚Äã NSE was able to sustain zero pricing only because of its financial strength and dominance in other segments, and that it was 
cross-subsidising losses in the CD segment to foreclose competition.  
134 
 
‚ùñ‚Äã The conduct was alleged to violate Sections 4(2)(a)(ii) (unfair / predatory pricing), 4(2)(b)(i) and (ii) (limiting production 
and technical development), 4(2)(c) (denial of market access), 4(2)(d) (imposition of supplementary obligations), and 4(2)(e) 
(leveraging dominance in one market to protect another). 
 
Issues: 
‚ùñ‚Äã What is the relevant market for assessing dominance in the present case? 
‚ùñ‚Äã Whether NSE is dominant in the relevant market within the meaning of Section 4 read with Explanation (a)? 
‚ùñ‚Äã Whether NSE‚Äôs zero-pricing strategy in the CD segment amounts to predatory pricing under Section 4, Explanation (b)? 
‚ùñ‚Äã Whether NSE used its dominance in one relevant market to enter into or protect another market, thereby violating Section 
4(2)(e)? 
‚ùñ‚Äã Whether intent and recoupment [essential elements of predatory pricing] are established on facts? 
 
Application: 
‚ùñ‚Äã Relevant Market Determination 
‚û¢‚Äã Rejected the argument that the relevant market comprised all stock exchange services in India ‚Üí markets in the 
financial sector are segment-specific and functionally distinct. 
‚û¢‚Äã The CD segment of stock exchange services in India constituted the relevant product market ‚Üí caters to a distinct 
set of consumers, involves different trading instruments, and operates under a separate regulatory and economic 
framework.  
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Aso highlighted the fluid and dynamic nature of network industries, cautioning that excessive regulatory 
intervention in such markets may itself be counterproductive. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Whether NSE Is Dominant in the Relevant Market 
‚û¢‚Äã While NSE possessed significant financial resources, economies of scale, and vertical integration, these factors alone 
were insufficient to establish dominance. 
‚û¢‚Äã Importantly, the Commission noted that: 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Market shares of competitors in the CD segment (including MCX-SX and USE) showed effective 
competition. 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Entry barriers were not prohibitive. 
‚óè‚Äã
Requirements such as minimum members or infrastructure costs were functional necessities to 
ensure liquidity, not exclusionary barriers. 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
The ability to sustain zero pricing did not automatically translate into dominance, especially in a network 
industry where scale and liquidity evolve over time. 
‚û¢‚Äã The CCI clarified the meaning of ‚Äúin its favour‚Äù under Explanation (a).  
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Dominance implies the ability to stabilise or continuously increase market share by weakening 
competitive constraints.  
‚ñÝ‚Äã
On the facts, NSE was neither operating independently of competitive forces nor influencing the market 
in its favour. 
‚û¢‚Äã Accordingly, NSE was held not dominant in the relevant market. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Whether Zero Pricing Amounts to Predatory Pricing 
‚û¢‚Äã Predatory pricing involves a two-phase strategy: 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Predation phase: deliberate loss-making to eliminate rivals, followed by 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Recoupment phase: raising prices to recover losses after rivals exit. 
‚û¢‚Äã Low prices, even prices below cost, do not by themselves constitute predation, even if some competitors exit or 
decide not to enter.  
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Exit in a market may be inefficient or unrelated to anti-competitive conduct. 
‚û¢‚Äã In network industries such as stock exchanges: 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
The initial value of the service is low due to lack of liquidity. 
‚óè‚Äã
Consumers are unwilling to pay prices based on cost considerations when trading volumes are 
low. 
135 
 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Pricing strategies are linked to network externalities, not short-term profit maximisation. 
‚û¢‚Äã The Commission accepted NSE‚Äôs justification that zero pricing was a commercially rational strategy to build 
liquidity and trading volumes in a nascent market.  
‚ñÝ‚Äã
The efficiencies arising from production and consumption externalities justified the pricing model. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Predatory Intent and Recoupment 
‚û¢‚Äã Intent is a crucial element of predatory pricing ‚Üí while intent may be inferred from conduct, it cannot be 
presumed solely from low pricing. 
‚û¢‚Äã On facts: 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
There was no realistic possibility of recoupment, since competition persisted even at zero prices. 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
The market structure remained contestable. 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
No evidence suggested that NSE intended to later raise prices to supra-competitive levels. 
‚û¢‚Äã Thus, both predatory intent and recoupment were absent. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Leveraging Under Section 4(2)(e) 
‚û¢‚Äã The Commission clarified that Section 4(2)(e) requires: 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Identification of two distinct relevant markets, 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Dominance in one of those markets, and 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Use of that dominance to enter or protect the second market in a manner that causes anti-competitive 
foreclosure. 
‚û¢‚Äã While involvement in multiple markets is common and often efficiency-enhancing, leveraging becomes abusive 
only when it results in consumer harm, not merely competitor disadvantage. 
‚û¢‚Äã The CCI held that: 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
NSE was not dominant in the CD segment. 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
No sufficient link or associative nexus was established between dominance in another market and alleged 
abuse in the CD segment. 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Mere cross-presence across markets does not satisfy Section 4(2)(e). 
‚û¢‚Äã Accordingly, Section 4(2)(e) was not attracted. 
 
Conclusion: 
‚ùñ‚Äã NSE was not dominant in the relevant market. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Zero pricing in the CD segment did not amount to predatory pricing. 
‚ùñ‚Äã No predatory intent or possibility of recoupment was established. 
‚ùñ‚Äã NSE had not leveraged dominance in one market to protect another under Section 4(2)(e). 
‚ùñ‚Äã All allegations of abuse of dominance were dismissed, and NSE was found not in violation of Section 4 of the Competition 
Act, 2002.

‚Äã Abuse of Dominance (Section 4)
‚Äã Section 4 does not prohibit dominance per se, but only abuse of dominance.
